post.content:
Round 1: Machine Coding

Given list of folders, print the path of a given folder from root. If there is no path to the root folder then return an empty string. Root level folders will have 0 as id. The structure of Folder is like this:
 class Folder {
 int id;
 List<int> subfolders;
 String name;
 }

 Ex:
 list = [Folder(0, [7, 3], “abc”), Folder(0, [], “xyz”), Folder(3, [], “pqr”), Folder(8, [], “def”), Folder(7, [9], “ijk), Folder(9, [], “lmn”)]

 /abc/ijk/lmn
 /abc/pqr
 /xyz
 ?? def/


 printPath(9) = “abc” -> “ijk” -> “lmn” printPath(8) = ""
 Clarification: There can be multiple root level folders. All other folders have unique ids.
 Note: printPath method may be called multiple times concurrently. Design your solution taking that into account
 Concurrency in input and printPath()

 addFolder(Folder f)
 printPath(int index)
 
 Round 2 (DSA): Given an n-ary tree, print the boundary nodes. Was doable using BFS.
 Given a list  of string which can contain any characters, write functions to serialize it to a file and then desiarlize the file back into the list.
 
 Round 3 (SD): Design whatsapp.
 
 Round 4 (HM): Discussions on past projects and their designs. Challenges and questions on opeational excellence. Some behavioural questions.
 
 Round 5 (BR): Discussions on previous projects. Effeciency and operational excellance.
 
 
 Rejected as the recruiter told me that I had all positives except the machine coding one. Can someone help me with the machine coding question.
Question 1) can be simple done using DFS traversal of n-ary tree.  We need to build one n-ary tree. Let me know how you solved it :)

`
      from __future__ import annotations
    from ast import Str


    class Folder :
        def __init__(self, id, name) :
            self.id = id
            self.name = name
            self.folders = []
            self.parentFolder = None
        
        def addChildFolder(self, folderId):
            self.folders.append(folderId)
        
        def assignParentFolder(self, folder:Folder):
            self.parentFolder = folder
        

    class FolderManager:
        def __init__(self) -> None:
            self.foldersMap = {}
        
        def addFolder(self, folder:Folder):
            self.foldersMap[folder.id] = folder

        def getFolderPath(self, folderId, path:Str):
            if folderId == 0:
                return path
            folder = self.foldersMap[folderId]
            if folder.parentFolder is not None:
                path = folder.parentFolder.name + "/ " + path 
                return self.getFolderPath(folder.parentFolder.id, path )
            else:
                return path
        
    folderManager = FolderManager()
    folder1 = Folder(0, "abc")
    folder2 = Folder(0, "xyz")
    folder3 = Folder(3, "pqr")
    folder3.assignParentFolder(folder1)
    folder8 = Folder(8, "def")
    folder7 = Folder(7, "ijk")
    folder7.assignParentFolder(folder1)
    folder9 = Folder(9, "lmn")
    folder9.assignParentFolder(folder7)

    folder1.addChildFolder(folder7.id)
    folder1.addChildFolder(folder3.id)
    folder7.addChildFolder(folder9.id)

    folderManager.addFolder(folder1)
    folderManager.addFolder(folder2)
    folderManager.addFolder(folder3)
    folderManager.addFolder(folder8)
    folderManager.addFolder(folder7)
    folderManager.addFolder(folder9)

    print(folderManager.getFolderPath(3, folder3.name))
    print(folderManager.getFolderPath(9, folder9.name))
    print(folderManager.getFolderPath(8, folder8.name))
    print(folderManager.getFolderPath(7, folder7.name))


        




        

        


`

For round 1, not sure if this is the cleanest option but you could store the folders in a Trie data structure. And, whenever you add a folder, you can keep a note of the path you are traversing from the root. After adding the folder, you can store the index and path in a map for easier retrieval.
